content:
# Exodus Chapter 27 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~48r–49r (PDF pp. 94–96); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 69–70 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_027_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_027_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_027_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_027_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Altar of Burnt Offering (vv. 1–8)

**1** "And you shall make [**ve'asita**] the altar [**et-hammizbeyach**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**]: five [**chamesh**] cubits [**ammot**] the length [**orekh**] and five [**chamesh**] cubits [**ammot**] the width [**rochav**]; the altar [**hammizbeyach**] shall be square [**ravua**], and three [**veshalosh**] cubits [**ammot**] its height [**qomato**].

**2** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] its horns [**qarnotav**] on [**al**] its four [**arba**] corners [**pinnotav**]; from it [**mimmenu**] shall its horns [**qarnotav**] be [**tihyeynah**], and you shall overlay [**vetsippita**] it [**oto**] with bronze [**nechoshet**].

**3** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] its pots [**sirotav**] to receive its ashes [**ledassheno**], and its shovels [**veya'av**], and its basins [**umizraqotav**], and its forks [**umizlegotav**], and its firepans [**umachtotav**]; all [**lekhol**] its vessels [**kelav**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] of bronze [**nechoshet**].

**4** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] for it [**lo**] a grating [**mikhbar**], a network [**ma'aseh**] of bronze [**nechoshet**] mesh [**reshet**]; and you shall make [**ve'asita**] upon [**al**] the network [**hareshet**] four [**arba**] rings [**tabb'ot**] of bronze [**nechoshet**] on [**al**] its four [**arba**] ends [**qetsotav**].

**5** And you shall set [**venatattha**] it [**otah**] under [**tachat**] the ledge [**karkov**] of the altar [**hammizbeyach**] below [**millematah**], and the network [**hareshet**] shall reach [**vehay'tah**] to [**ad**] the middle [**chatsi**] of the altar [**hammizbeyach**].

**6** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] poles [**baddim**] for the altar [**lammizbeyach**], poles [**baddim**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**], and you shall overlay [**vetsippita**] them [**otam**] with bronze [**nechoshet**].

**7** And its poles [**baddav**] shall be put [**vehuva**] into the rings [**battabb'ot**], and the poles [**habaddim**] shall be [**vehay'u**] on [**al**] the two [**shney**] sides [**tsal'ot**] of the altar [**hammizbeyach**] when it is carried [**bisse't**] it [**oto**].

**8** Hollow [**nevuv**] with boards [**luchot**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] it [**oto**]; as [**ka'asher**] He showed [**her'ah**] you [**otekha**] on the mountain [**bahar**], so [**ken**] shall they make [**ya'asu**] it.

---

## The Courtyard of the Tabernacle (vv. 9–19)

**9** "And you shall make [**ve'asita**] the courtyard [**et chatsar**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**]: for the south side [**lif'at**] southward [**negev teymanah**], hangings [**qela'im**] for the courtyard [**lechatser**] of fine twisted linen [**shesh moshzar**], one hundred [**me'ah**] cubits [**ba'ammah**] in length [**orekh**] for one [**ha'echad**] side [**happe'ah**];

**10** and its pillars [**ve'ammudav**] shall be twenty [**esrim**], and their bases [**ve'adneyhem**] twenty [**esrim**], of bronze [**nechoshet**]; the hooks [**vavey**] of the pillars [**ha'ammudim**] and their bands [**vachashuqeyhem**] shall be of silver [**khesef**].

**11** And likewise [**vekhen**] for the north [**tsafon**] side [**lif'at**] in length [**ba'orekh**], hangings [**qela'im**] one hundred [**me'ah**] cubits in length [**orekh**], and its pillars [**ve'ammudav**] twenty [**esrim**], and their bases [**ve'adneyhem**] twenty [**esrim**], of bronze [**nechoshet**]; the hooks [**vavey**] of the pillars [**ha'ammudim**] and their bands [**vachashuqeyhem**] shall be of silver [**khesef**].

**12** And the width [**verochav**] of the courtyard [**hechatser**] on the west [**yam**] side [**lif'at**] shall have hangings [**qela'im**] of fifty [**chamishim**] cubits [**ammah**]; their pillars [**ammudeyhem**] ten [**asarah**] and their bases [**ve'adneyhem**] ten [**asarah**].

**13** And the width [**verochav**] of the courtyard [**hechatser**] on the east side [**lif'at**] eastward [**qedmah mizrachah**] shall be fifty [**chamishim**] cubits [**ammah**].

**14** And the hangings [**qela'im**] for the one [**ha'echad**] shoulder [**hakkathef**] shall be fifteen [**chamishah asar**] cubits [**ammah**]; their pillars [**ammudeyhem**] three [**sheloshah**] and their bases [**ve'adneyhem**] three [**sheloshah**].

**15** And for the second [**hasshenit**] shoulder [**hakkathef**], hangings [**qela'im**] of fifteen [**chamishah esreh**] cubits; their pillars [**ammudeyhem**] three [**sheloshah**] and their bases [**ve'adneyhem**] three [**sheloshah**].

**16** And for the gate [**uleshar**] of the courtyard [**hechatser**] shall be a screen [**masakh**] of twenty [**esrim**] cubits [**ammah**], of blue [**tekheleth**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet yarn [**vetola'at shani**] and fine twisted linen [**veshesh moshzar**], the work [**ma'aseh**] of an embroiderer [**roqem**]; their pillars [**ammudeyhem**] four [**arba'ah**] and their bases [**ve'adneyhem**] four [**arba'ah**].

**17** All [**kol**] the pillars [**ammudey**] of the courtyard [**hechatser**] all around [**saviv**] shall be banded [**machushaqim**] with silver [**khesef**]; their hooks [**vaveyhem**] shall be of silver [**khesef**] and their bases [**ve'adneyhem**] of bronze [**nechoshet**].

**18** The length [**orekh**] of the courtyard [**hechatser**] shall be one hundred [**me'ah**] cubits [**ba'ammah**], and the width [**verochav**] fifty [**chamishim**] by fifty [**bachamishim**], and the height [**veqomah**] five [**chamesh**] cubits [**ammot**] of fine twisted linen [**shesh moshzar**], and their bases [**ve'adneyhem**] of bronze [**nechoshet**].

**19** All [**lekhol**] the vessels [**keley**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**] for all [**bekhol**] its service [**avodato**], and all [**vekhol**] its tent pegs [**yetedotav**], and all [**vekhol**] the tent pegs [**yitdot**] of the courtyard [**hechatser**] shall be of bronze [**nechoshet**].

---

## Oil for the Lamp (vv. 20–21)

**20** "And you [**ve'attah**] shall command [**tetsavveh**] the Sons of Israel [**et-beney Yisra'el**] that they bring [**veyiqchu**] to you [**eleykha**] pure [**zakh**] oil [**shemen**] of beaten [**katit**] olives [**zayit**] for the light [**lamma'or**], to cause a lamp [**ner**] to go up [**leha'alot**] continually [**tamid**].

**21** In the Tent [**be'ohel**] of Meeting [**mo'ed**], outside [**michutz**] the veil [**lapparokhet**] which [**asher**] is before [**al**] the testimony [**ha'edut**], Aaron [**Aharon**] and his sons [**uvanav**] shall arrange [**ya'arokh**] it [**oto**] from evening [**me'erev**] until [**ad**] morning [**boqer**] before [**lifney**] YHWH; a statute [**chuqqat**] forever [**olam**] for their generations [**ledorotam**] from [**me'et**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**mizbeyach** (vv. 1–8) -- "altar." The noun mizbeyach (from z-v-ch, "to slaughter, sacrifice") denotes the place where sacrifices are offered -- literally "a place of slaughtering." This is the altar of burnt offering (mizbeyach ha'olah), the large outdoor altar that stands in the courtyard before the entrance to the Tabernacle. It is distinct from the smaller golden altar of incense (mizbeyach haqqetoret) described in Exodus 30. The altar is constructed of acacia wood but overlaid with bronze (nechoshet), reflecting its position in the outer courtyard -- the zone of bronze rather than gold. Its dimensions (five cubits by five cubits, three cubits high) make it the largest piece of furniture in the Tabernacle complex. The square shape (ravua) symbolizes completeness and stability. The LXX renders mizbeyach as thusiastērion ("altar, place of sacrifice"), the term that carries through the New Testament for both the literal altar (Matt 5:23-24) and the metaphorical altar of Christ's sacrifice (Heb 13:10).

**qarnot** (v. 2) -- "horns." The noun qeren (plural qarnot) denotes the projections rising from the four corners of the altar. These horns are integral to the altar ("from it shall its horns be," mimmenu tihyeynah qarnotav) -- not attached separately but formed from the altar structure itself. The horns serve multiple ritual functions: blood is applied to them during sin offerings (Lev 4:7, 18, 25, 30); they serve as a place of asylum for fugitives seeking sanctuary (1 Kgs 1:50-51; 2:28); and they represent the power and authority of the altar. The horn (qeren) is a pervasive biblical symbol of strength and power (Deut 33:17; 1 Sam 2:1, 10; Ps 75:5-6). That the altar's horns rise at the four corners -- the four directions of the compass -- suggests the universal reach of the sacrifice offered upon it. The LXX renders qarnot as kerata ("horns"), and the Vaticanus text clearly preserves this reading.

**mikhbar ma'aseh reshet nechoshet** (v. 4) -- "a grating, a network of bronze mesh." The noun mikhbar (from k-v-r, "to interweave, plait") denotes a grating or lattice-work, and reshet ("net, network") further specifies the open mesh construction. This bronze grating is placed within the altar, below the ledge (karkov), reaching to the midpoint of the altar's height. Its function is debated: it may have served as a support for the firewood and sacrifice above, allowed air to circulate for combustion, or caught ash and embers falling from the fire above. The grating with its four bronze rings also provides the attachment points for the carrying poles, positioning them at the altar's center of gravity. The LXX renders mikhbar as escharas ("hearth, grating") and reshet as diktuōton ("netlike"), accurately conveying the mesh construction.

**karkov** (v. 5) -- "ledge, rim." The noun karkov (from k-r-k, "to surround, encircle") denotes a projecting ledge or rim that runs around the exterior of the altar, apparently at its midpoint. The karkov may have served as a walkway or platform for the priests to stand upon while ministering at the altar, given that the altar is three cubits (approximately 4.5 feet / 1.4 meters) high. The bronze grating is set below this ledge. The term occurs only in descriptions of the altar and is rendered in the LXX as a part of the altar's structural framework. The Vaticanus text preserves this as part of the altar construction instructions.

**chatser** (vv. 9–19) -- "courtyard, enclosure." The noun chatser (from ch-ts-r, "to enclose, surround") denotes the open-air rectangular enclosure surrounding the Tabernacle. Its dimensions are 100 cubits long by 50 cubits wide (approximately 150 feet by 75 feet / 46 meters by 23 meters), oriented east to west, with the Tabernacle positioned in the western half. The courtyard is defined by linen hangings (qela'im) suspended from pillars set in bronze bases, creating a fabric-walled enclosure approximately 7.5 feet / 2.3 meters high. The chatser marks the outermost boundary of sacred space -- the threshold between the common and the holy. Only Israelites in a state of ritual purity could enter the courtyard; the priests served at the altar within it; and only the priests could enter the Tabernacle itself beyond the courtyard. The LXX renders chatser as aulē ("courtyard"), the term used in the New Testament for the temple courts (Matt 26:3; John 18:15).

**qela'im** (vv. 9–15) -- "hangings, curtain-screens." The noun qela (plural qela'im, from q-l-', "to hang, sling") denotes the linen fabric panels that form the walls of the courtyard. These are distinct from the yeri'ot ("curtains") of the Tabernacle structure (ch. 26) -- the qela'im are simpler hangings of plain fine twisted linen (shesh moshzar) without the colored yarn or cherubim designs of the inner curtains. They are purely white linen, creating a boundary of ritual purity around the sacred precinct. The whiteness of the linen walls would have been visually striking against the desert landscape, marking the courtyard as a space set apart. The LXX renders qela'im as istia ("sails, canvas"), evoking the image of sailcloth stretched between masts -- an apt comparison for the linen panels stretched between their pillars.

**kathef** (vv. 14–15) -- "shoulder." The noun kathef (literally "shoulder") is used architecturally to denote the side panels flanking a gate or doorway. The eastern side of the courtyard, where the entrance gate is located, is divided into three sections: a fifteen-cubit hanging on the north shoulder, a fifteen-cubit hanging on the south shoulder, and a twenty-cubit embroidered screen for the gate in the center. The total (15 + 15 + 20 = 50 cubits) matches the courtyard's width. The metaphorical use of "shoulder" for the flanking walls evokes the image of the gateway as a body, with the screen as its face and the side hangings as its shoulders. The LXX renders kathef as klitos ("side, flank").

**masakh** (v. 16) -- "screen." See the note on masakh at 26:36. Here the same term describes the embroidered screen at the courtyard gate. Like the Tabernacle entrance screen, this is the work of a roqem ("embroiderer") and employs the full Tabernacle color palette (blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen). The courtyard screen marks the first threshold of sacred space: passing through it, one moves from the profane to the holy. It is supported by four pillars with four bronze bases -- fewer and simpler than the Tabernacle entrance screen's five pillars, consistent with the gradient of sanctity moving inward.

**shemen zayit zakh katit** (v. 20) -- "pure oil of beaten olives." The phrase describes olive oil of the highest quality, produced by crushing (katit, from k-t-t, "to beat, crush") the olives in a mortar rather than pressing them in a press. The first oil that flows from crushed olives is the purest, clearest (zakh, "pure, clear, bright"), and most free of sediment. This cold-pressed first extraction was reserved for sacred use -- for the lamp of the Tabernacle. Later pressings, which yielded greater quantity but lesser quality, were used for cooking and other purposes. The requirement for beaten rather than pressed oil reflects the principle that what is offered to God must be the firstfruits, the best, the most carefully prepared. The LXX renders shemen zayit zakh katit as elaion ex elaiōn atrugiton katharon ("oil from olives, pure, without sediment"), and the Vaticanus text preserves this expanded rendering.

**ner tamid** (v. 20) -- "a perpetual lamp" or "a lamp continually." The phrase leha'alot ner tamid ("to cause a lamp to go up continually") has given rise to the synagogue tradition of the ner tamid, the "eternal light" that burns perpetually before the Ark in every synagogue. However, the Hebrew tamid here means "regularly, continually" rather than "eternally without ceasing" -- as verse 21 specifies, the lamp is tended "from evening until morning," suggesting it was lit at dusk and burned through the night. The verb leha'alot (Hiphil infinitive construct of '-l-h, "to go up, ascend") literally means "to cause to go up" -- referring to the flame ascending from the lamp's wick. This verb for lighting a lamp is distinctive: one "raises up" a flame rather than "igniting" or "kindling" it, lending a sense of elevation and offering to the act of lighting. The LXX renders tamid as dia pantos ("through all [time], continually").

### Grammatical Notes

**mimmenu tihyeynah qarnotav** (v. 2) -- "from it shall its horns be." The preposition min ("from") combined with the third masculine singular suffix (mimmenu, "from it") indicates that the horns are to be made from the same piece as the altar itself -- they are organically connected, not separate attachments. The verb tihyeynah is the Qal imperfect third feminine plural of h-y-h ("to be"), agreeing with qarnotav ("its horns," feminine plural). The theological implication is significant: the horns -- the seat of atoning blood, the place of refuge -- are not accessories added to the altar but are intrinsic to its nature. The altar is, by its very constitution, a place of power and a place of asylum.

**nevuv luchot ta'aseh oto** (v. 8) -- "hollow with boards you shall make it." The adjective nevuv ("hollow, empty") from n-v-v indicates that the altar is not a solid block of acacia wood but a hollow frame overlaid with bronze, presumably filled with earth or stones when set up for use (cf. Exod 20:24, where God commands an altar of earth). The noun luchot ("boards, planks") describes the flat panels forming the sides of this hollow structure. The acacia wood frame with bronze overlay thus serves as a portable shell; the actual altar surface on which fire burns may have been the earth or stones placed inside. This resolves the apparent difficulty of burning sacrifices on a wooden altar -- the wood is not the hearth but the frame. The LXX renders nevuv as koilon ("hollow"), confirming this understanding.

**ka'asher her'ah otekha bahar ken ya'asu** (v. 8) -- "as He showed you on the mountain, so shall they make it." The Hiphil perfect of r-'a-h ("to see") with the third masculine singular subject ("He showed") and the second masculine singular object ("you") recalls the recurring Sinai-pattern motif (25:9, 40; 26:30). The shift to the third person plural ya'asu ("they shall make") is notable: while the instructions are addressed to Moses in the second person singular ("you shall make"), the actual construction will be carried out by the community, particularly by Bezalel and Oholiab (31:1-6). Moses receives the vision; the craftsmen execute it. This division between the one who sees the pattern and those who build according to it underscores Moses' unique mediatorial role.

**ya'arokh oto me'erev ad boqer** (v. 21) -- "he shall arrange it from evening until morning." The verb ya'arokh (Qal imperfect third masculine singular of '-r-k, "to arrange, set in order") is the same verb used for arranging the showbread on the table (Lev 24:8) and for setting wood and sacrifice in order on the altar (Lev 1:7-8). The subject "Aaron and his sons" takes a singular verb (ya'arokh) because the duty falls to the priestly office collectively, represented by whoever serves on a given evening. The time frame "from evening until morning" (me'erev ad boqer) specifies that the lamp burns during the hours of darkness, when the Tabernacle interior would otherwise be entirely without light. The lamp ensures that the divine presence is never in darkness -- or perhaps more precisely, that the priestly service of God's presence never ceases, even through the night.

**chuqqat olam ledorotam me'et beney Yisra'el** (v. 21) -- "a statute forever for their generations from the Sons of Israel." The construct chain chuqqat olam ("statute of perpetuity") declares that this commandment is not temporary but endures through all generations. The preposition me'et ("from") before beney Yisra'el indicates that the oil is to be supplied "from" the people -- it is the congregation's responsibility to provide the lamp oil, even though the priests tend the lamp. The perpetual nature of this statute extends the lamp's significance beyond the wilderness period: it was observed in Solomon's temple, in the Second Temple, and its echo persists in the synagogue's ner tamid to this day.

### Structural Notes

Exodus 27 completes the physical description of the Tabernacle complex that began in chapter 25. If chapter 25 described the interior furniture (Ark, Table, Lampstand) and chapter 26 described the Tabernacle structure itself (curtains, frames, veil, screen), chapter 27 moves outward to describe the courtyard and its principal fixture: the bronze altar of burnt offering. The chapter's movement is from center to periphery: (1) the bronze altar that stands in the courtyard before the Tabernacle entrance (vv. 1-8), (2) the courtyard enclosure defined by its hangings, pillars, and gate (vv. 9-19), and (3) the perpetual lamp that bridges the outer and inner sanctuaries (vv. 20-21).

The chapter's tripartite structure mirrors the three zones of sacred space: the Most Holy Place (where the Ark resides behind the veil), the Holy Place (where the Table, Lampstand, and eventually the incense altar stand), and the Courtyard (where the bronze altar stands and the people gather). Chapter 27 describes the outermost zone -- the zone of bronze rather than gold, of public sacrifice rather than hidden worship, of congregational assembly rather than priestly solitude.

The courtyard dimensions (100 cubits by 50 cubits) create a 2:1 rectangle with the same proportions as the Tabernacle itself (30 cubits by 10 cubits for the Holy Place plus Most Holy Place). This proportional harmony between the outer and inner structures is characteristic of sacred architecture across the ancient Near East: the smaller mirrors the larger, the inner replicates the outer, because all dimensions derive from a single divine pattern.

The final two verses (20-21) form a bridge between the construction instructions of chapters 25-27 and the priestly investiture instructions that will follow in chapter 28. The lamp oil command is the first instruction that concerns ongoing worship practice rather than initial construction. It introduces the role of Aaron and his sons for the first time in the Tabernacle legislation, preparing for the detailed priestly garment and consecration instructions of chapters 28-29. The lamp -- tended nightly by Aaron's priestly line, fueled by oil supplied by the congregation -- thus becomes a symbol of the perpetual covenant relationship between YHWH, the priesthood, and Israel.

### Theological Notes

The bronze altar is the first object encountered by anyone entering the courtyard, and it stands as the theological gateway to God's presence. Before one can approach the Holy Place, one must pass the altar -- the place where life is offered and blood is shed. The altar's position declares that access to God is mediated through sacrifice. This spatial theology finds its New Testament fulfillment in the cross: "We have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat" (Heb 13:10). Just as the bronze altar stood between the outer world and the Tabernacle, so the cross stands between humanity's fallen state and access to God's presence.

The horns of the altar (v. 2) concentrate multiple theological themes. As the place where atoning blood is applied (Lev 4), they represent the efficacy of sacrifice. As the place of refuge (1 Kgs 1:50), they represent God's mercy. As projections reaching upward from the four corners, they represent the universal scope and ascending power of the sacrifice. The insistence that the horns are "from it" (mimmenu) -- organically part of the altar, not additions -- teaches that the power of atonement and the offer of refuge are inherent to the sacrificial system itself, not supplementary features.

The courtyard enclosure (vv. 9-19) establishes the fundamental boundary between sacred and profane space. The white linen hangings, five cubits high, prevent the casual gaze from seeing the altar and its sacrifices. The boundary is real but permeable: there is a gate, and the gate is marked by an embroidered screen that both invites and regulates entry. The courtyard's single entrance on the east means that one always approaches God's presence by moving westward -- the direction of the setting sun, the direction of journey's end. The Tabernacle entrance, the veil, and the Ark all lie on this single east-west axis, creating a liturgical path that moves progressively deeper into holiness.

The lamp oil command (vv. 20-21) introduces the theme of Israel's ongoing participation in maintaining God's dwelling. The Tabernacle is not merely built by the people; it is sustained by them. The pure beaten olive oil, supplied perpetually by the congregation, keeps the light burning in God's house. The lamp that burns "from evening until morning" ensures that even in the darkness, the sanctuary is not abandoned. This is a theology of faithful presence -- of showing up, night after night, to tend the flame. The priestly duty of arranging the lamp becomes a paradigm for all worship: regular, faithful, undramatic, essential. The light in the sanctuary is not for God's benefit (He does not need illumination) but for the sake of the testimony -- it burns "before YHWH," as an act of devotion and a sign that the covenant people have not forgotten their God, even in the dark watches of the night.

The phrase "before YHWH" (lifney YHWH, v. 21) marks the lamp-tending as a liturgical act performed in the divine presence. Though Aaron stands outside the veil (michutz lapparokhet), he stands "before YHWH" -- the boundary of the veil does not negate the reality of divine presence in the outer chamber. The lamp burns before the testimony (ha'edut), the covenant terms housed in the Ark beyond the veil. Light before the testimony: illumination before the word of God. The association of light with God's revealed will anticipates Psalm 119:105 -- "Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path" -- and ultimately the Johannine identification of Christ as both the Word and the Light (John 1:1-9).

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 27 (pages 69-70) preserves the LXX rendering with substantial fidelity to the MT, though with several characteristic features. At v. 1, the LXX renders mizbeyach as thusiastērion ("altar of sacrifice") and specifies the acacia wood (xulōn asēptōn, "incorruptible wood"). At v. 2, the horns (kerata) are described as being ek tōn tessarōn gōniōn ("from the four corners"), faithfully rendering the MT's al arba pinnotav. At v. 3, the altar vessels are enumerated with slight variations: the LXX includes stephane ("wreath, crown") for the altar's rim, a detail that may reflect an interpretive expansion or a variant tradition. At v. 4, the bronze grating is rendered as escharas diktuōton ("grating of netlike work"), closely matching the MT's mikhbar ma'aseh reshet. At v. 5, the karkov is integrated into the altar's structural description. At v. 8, the command to build according to the mountain pattern is rendered kata to paradeigma to dedeigmenon soi en tō orei ("according to the pattern shown to you on the mountain"), using paradeigma ("pattern, model") where 25:9 used paradeigma for the Hebrew tavnit. At vv. 9-19, the courtyard (aulē) description follows the MT closely, with the pillars (stuloi), bases (baseis), and hangings (istia, "sails/canvas") enumerated systematically. At v. 16, the embroidered gate screen is described as poikilton ("of variegated work"), distinguishing the roqem-work from the choshev-work. At v. 17, the silver-banded pillars are described with the LXX's characteristic perikechrusōmenai argurō ("overlaid with silver"), a more explicit rendering of the MT's machushaqim khesef. At vv. 20-21, the lamp oil instructions are rendered faithfully: elaion ex elaiōn atrugiton katharon ("oil from olives, pure, without sediment") for shemen zayit zakh katit, and the perpetual statute (nomimon aiōnion, "an eternal ordinance") for chuqqat olam. The Vaticanus manuscript shows Exodus 27 continuing from the lower portion of page 69 across the column break onto page 70, with the courtyard description and lamp oil command occupying the upper and middle columns of page 70.

--- LOGOS
